summary:
Hernán Cortés's arrival in Mexico (1519 AD) initiated one of history's most consequential — and most contested — encounters between civilizations, as a small force of approximately 600 Spanish soldiers, 16 horses, and 14 cannon began the process that would end the [[event:2319|Aztec Empire]] and transform the Americas. Cortés landed on the Yucatán coast in February 1519, acquired the enslaved interpreter La Malinche (Malintzin/Doña Marina) whose knowledge of Nahuatl and Spanish became essential, and began his advance toward Tenochtitlán — at the time one of the world's largest cities, home to perhaps 200,000-300,000 people. Cortés's advance was facilitated by the profound resentments many peoples felt toward Aztec dominance: the Tlaxcalans, after initially opposing the Spaniards, became essential military allies.

The reception in Tenochtitlán was extraordinary: Emperor Moctezuma II admitted Cortés and his force into the city, apparently viewing them with a complex mixture of diplomatic calculation, spiritual uncertainty (the Quetzalcóatl myth — that Cortés was a returning deity — has been contested by modern scholars as a post-conquest narrative), and political miscalculation about the Spaniards' intentions. The Spanish and their allies were admitted to the city, quartered in royal palaces, and initially treated as honored guests — before Cortés took Moctezuma hostage, triggering the crisis that would escalate into the [[event:3625|fall of Tenochtitlán]]. Cortés's personal audacity in burning his own ships to prevent retreat, his exploitation of Indigenous political divisions, and his systematic diplomatic and military skill represented the conquistador tradition at its most formidable.
